Success criteria met: GC, disk-full handling, seed hygiene (y4m.7 gated go-live — landed), gateway failover, preflight, IPC hardening, backups, logging, CLI contract all shipped with tests.

title: Make money-DB synchronous explicit (PRAGMA synchronous=FULL)
description:
# PR-7 (HARDEN) — Make money-DB synchronous explicit (PRAGMA synchronous=FULL)

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-7.

## Problem / evidence
lnrent seeds an ecosystem of independent third-party operators — the operator is a
stranger who inherits whatever sqlite the box happens to ship. `store.rs::open` sets only
WAL + foreign_keys and never states its durability level:

```
daemon/src/store.rs:409  pub fn open(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> Result<Connection> {
daemon/src/store.rs:410      let conn = Connection::open(path)?;
daemon/src/store.rs:411      conn.execute_batch("PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL; PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON;")?;
daemon/src/store.rs:412      migrate(&conn)?;
```

The original data-loss claim (WAL-default-`NORMAL`) is **VERIFIED FALSE and must not be
reintroduced:** this build uses rusqlite `bundled` (libsqlite3-sys 0.28), which compiles the
amalgamation with neither `SQLITE_DEFAULT_SYNCHRONOUS` nor `SQLITE_DEFAULT_WAL_SYNCHRONOUS`
overridden, so the WAL default is already **FULL** (`synchronous == 2`). No current gap.

The residual, cheap risk: nothing pins that. A future switch to a system/distro sqlite —
where a `NORMAL-in-WAL` build default is common — would silently lower money-write durability
with no test to catch it.

## Design / fix
Add an explicit `PRAGMA synchronous=FULL` to the `store.rs::open` pragma batch (one line,
alongside the existing WAL/foreign_keys). No behavior change on the bundled build (already
FULL); it makes durability a stated invariant instead of a build-time default. Preserves the
sole-writer actor model — it is the same single connection.

## Acceptance criteria
- `open()` issues `PRAGMA synchronous=FULL`; money writes are FULL-synced regardless of the
  underlying sqlite build's default.
- Unit tests: open a fresh DB and assert `PRAGMA synchronous` returns **exactly 2** (the
  boundary — assert `== 2`, not `>= 1`, so a silent drop to `NORMAL`(1) fails the test).
- e2e / integration: not warranted — a single pragma with a direct unit assertion.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean

## Non-goals / scope discipline
Do NOT reintroduce the verified-false data-loss narrative. Do not touch `journal_mode`, add a
config knob for synchronous level, or tune durability for any non-money DB. One pragma + one
assertion — nothing more.

## Dependencies
-
close_reason: Merged as PR #39 (squash): explicit PRAGMA synchronous=FULL in store.rs::open pins money-write durability as a stated invariant (no-op on bundled sqlite which already defaults FULL, but a future system-sqlite switch can't silently lower it); test asserts PRAGMA synchronous == exactly 2

title: Close IPC socket bind-then-chmod window (+ optional peer-cred)
description:
# PR-15 (HARDEN) — close the IPC socket bind→chmod window (+ optional peer-cred)

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-15; focused spec: none.

## Problem / evidence
The IPC socket is the operator's control channel; authz is purely filesystem permissions. The socket
is bound, THEN chmod'd in a separate step — between the two it carries umask-default perms:
```
daemon/src/ipc.rs:109-113
    let _ = std::fs::remove_file(path);
    let listener =
        UnixListener::bind(path).with_context(|| format!("binding {}", path.display()))?;
    std::fs::set_permissions(path, std::fs::Permissions::from_mode(0o600))
        .with_context(|| format!("perms on {}", path.display()))?;
```
There is no `SO_PEERCRED` check — any process that can reach the socket in that window can issue
operator commands (suspend/resume/sweep/etc.). Narrow (needs a loose umask plus a pre-existing
world-traversable data dir, which `config.rs` deliberately permits), but a local user could act as
the operator in the gap. For a stranger-operator's unattended box this is a real local-privilege gap.

## Design / fix
Minimal, preserving the existing wire protocol and the graceful-shutdown accept-loop behavior:
- Set a tight `umask` (e.g. `0o177`) immediately BEFORE `bind` so the socket is created 0600 from the
  instant it exists (restore the prior umask right after), OR bind inside a private `0700`
  subdirectory of the data dir so the pre-bind window is unreachable regardless of umask. Keep the
  explicit `set_permissions(0o600)` as belt-and-suspenders.
- Optionally add an `SO_PEERCRED` check on accept to authorize by peer uid (defense-in-depth beyond
  FS perms). Keep it optional/secondary to the umask fix.

## Acceptance criteria
- The socket is never observable with looser-than-0600 perms, even under a loose process umask
  (no bind→chmod window).
- Existing IPC clients (operator CLI, buyer flows that don't touch this socket) still connect;
  wire protocol unchanged.
- Unit tests: after `serve_with_shutdown` binds, the socket's mode is 0600 under a deliberately-loose
  umask; (if peer-cred added) a connection from a disallowed uid is refused.
- e2e / integration where warranted: the existing IPC/admin-command tests stay green through the
  bind path change, with the bind sequence logged.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean.

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No auth tokens / TLS on the local socket; no move to TCP; no change to the IPC command surface. Do
NOT fold in the read-deadline fix — that is the sibling bead PR-24. Keep this to the bind→chmod race
(and optional peer-cred) only.

## Coupled edits / dependencies
PR-24 (IPC read deadline) is a SEPARATE sibling bead in the same `ipc.rs` file and EXTENDS this one;
sequence PR-24 after PR-15 to avoid overlapping edits to the connection path.
close_reason: Merged as PR #34 (squash): staged atomic 0700-dir bind + chmod-while-private + rename(2) publish (no perms window, no missing-path window), unsafe-squatter eviction (loose socket/file/symlink), SO_PEERCRED gate (daemon euid or root, fail closed), up-front sun_path validation (codex P2)

title: Optional structured (JSON) logging subscriber
description:
# PR-19 (HARDEN) — optional structured (JSON) logging subscriber

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-19; focused spec: none.

## Problem / evidence
A stranger-operator running unattended will want to ship logs to Loki/ELK for log-based alerting,
but the daemon emits unstructured plaintext despite carrying rich structured fields on money events:
```
daemon/src/main.rs:160
    tracing_subscriber::fmt::init();
```
`fmt::init()` is the human-readable text formatter, so the structured `tracing` fields on
money-critical events are flattened into prose — awkward to parse downstream.

## Design / fix
Add an opt-in JSON subscriber: gate on an env var / config flag (e.g. `LNRENT_LOG_FORMAT=json`,
default `text` = today's behavior) and, when set, initialize `tracing_subscriber::fmt().json()`
instead of the default text formatter. No change to what is logged or to log levels/`RUST_LOG` — only
the output encoding. Keep the plaintext default so nothing in go-live.md's log-watching guidance
changes for operators who don't opt in.

## Acceptance criteria
- Default (flag unset) behavior is byte-for-byte the current plaintext logging.
- With the flag set, log lines are valid JSON objects preserving the structured money-event fields.
- Unit tests: the format selector maps unset/`text` → text subscriber and `json` → JSON subscriber
  (test the selection function, not the global subscriber init).
- e2e / integration where warranted: none required (pure observability, no runtime-behavior surface)
  beyond a smoke assertion that the daemon boots under `LNRENT_LOG_FORMAT=json`.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean.

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No log shipping/agent, no metrics/Prometheus, no OpenTelemetry, no new alerting. This is strictly an
opt-in encoding switch. It is SECONDARY to PR-5's push alerts — JSON logs are a pull/scrape path, not
a substitute for the alert sink; do not build alerting here.

## Coupled edits / dependencies
None. Complements (does not replace) PR-5's alert dispatcher.
close_reason: Merged as PR #42 (squash): opt-in LNRENT_LOG_FORMAT=json selects tracing fmt().json(); unset/text/typo -> byte-identical text fmt::init(); pure parse_log_format selector tested

title: Process-group kill for timed-out hooks (no orphaned droplet)
description:
# PR-23 (HARDEN) — process-group kill for timed-out hooks (no orphaned billed droplet)

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-23 (2026-07-05 second pass, feeds PR-6); focused spec: none.

## Problem / evidence
For a stranger-operator running unattended against real fiat, an invisible billed droplet is exactly
the failure mode PR-6 exists to prevent — but a timed-out hook can create one PR-6 never sees. `reap`
SIGKILLs only the immediate hook process, not its descendants:
```
daemon/src/runner.rs:127-130
async fn reap(child: &mut tokio::process::Child) {
    let _ = child.start_kill();
    let _ = tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(5), child.wait()).await;
}
```
A timed-out do-vps provision's in-flight `curl` (spawned by the hook shell) survives the SIGKILL of
the shell and can complete the droplet-create AFTER the daemon declared failure, ran the (empty-by-
tag) cleanup, and refunded — an orphaned droplet that keeps costing money and that even PR-6's
dead-letter never records, because no hook ever reported a handle for it.

## Design / fix
Minimal and self-contained in `runner.rs`:
- At spawn, put the hook in its own process group: add `.process_group(0)` to the `Command` builder
  in `spawn_hook` (`runner.rs:104-110`) so the shell and every child (`curl`) share a pgid.
- In `reap`, kill the whole group (`killpg(pgid, SIGKILL)` / `libc::killpg`) instead of only the
  immediate child, then wait as today. Preserve the existing bounded 5s reap wait and the
  `kill_on_drop` backstop.
Keep the ETXTBSY/EAGAIN spawn-retry and output-cap behavior untouched — this changes only how the
timed-out process tree is torn down.

## Acceptance criteria
- A hook that spawns a long-lived child and then times out leaves NO surviving descendant after
  `reap` (the whole group is killed), verified without relying on the process exiting on its own.
- Normal (fast, clean-exit) hooks are unaffected; no zombies.
- Unit tests: a test hook that forks a sleeper child — after timeout+reap, the child pid is gone;
  existing runner tests (timeout, output cap, reap) stay green.
- e2e / integration where warranted: none strictly required; if a do-vps-shaped test exists, assert
  no orphaned curl survives a provision timeout, with the group-kill logged.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean.

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No change to hook timeouts, retry policy, or the dead-letter/PR-6 machinery itself; no cross-hook
supervision. Just the process-group spawn + group-kill on reap. Portability: Unix-only is fine
(the daemon is Unix-only).

## Coupled edits / dependencies
FEEDS PR-6 — this closes the gap where a timed-out provision produces a droplet PR-6's dead-letter
can never learn about; PR-6 remains the surfacing/retry path for teardown failures. No hard bead
dependency (this is worth landing on its own), but note the relationship in the PR-6 bead.
close_reason: Merged as PR #36 (squash): process_group(0) at spawn + libc::killpg(pgid,SIGKILL) in reap (pid before wait, ESRCH ignored) + HookProcessGroup RAII backstop (cancellation + clean-exit-failure); documented pgid-recycle residual; container-robust ESRCH-or-zombie test poll (codex P2)

title: IPC connection read deadline
description:
# PR-24 (HARDEN) — IPC connection read deadline (idle client can't wedge graceful drain)

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-24 (2026-07-05 second pass, extends PR-15); focused spec: none.

## Problem / evidence
`handle_conn` reads the request frame with no timeout:
```
daemon/src/ipc.rs:159-163
    let (rd, mut wr) = stream.into_split();
    // Bounded read: cap the request frame so an over-long line can't exhaust memory.
    let mut rd = BufReader::new(rd.take(MAX_REQUEST_BYTES));
    let mut line = String::new();
    rd.read_line(&mut line).await?;
```
The read is byte-bounded but not time-bounded. One idle connected client (it opens the socket and
never sends a line) pins the graceful IPC drain at shutdown: `serve_with_shutdown` awaits the
in-flight per-connection handlers so an admin txn+reply isn't lost, but an idle handler never
completes, so the drain waits until the shutdown's ~3s abort kills the whole task set — which can
abort a CONCURRENT in-flight handler after its transaction committed but before its reply was
written. For a money daemon that is a committed-but-unacknowledged admin action.

## Design / fix
Minimal, extends PR-15's hardening of the same connection path. Wrap the request read in a short
`tokio::time::timeout` (a few seconds — enough for any legitimate client to send its single line):
```
match tokio::time::timeout(READ_TIMEOUT, rd.read_line(&mut line)).await {
    Err(_elapsed) => reply bad_request ("request read timed out") and close,
    Ok(res)       => res?; … existing parse/dispatch …
}
```
On timeout, reply `bad_request` (mirroring the existing over-long/unterminated-frame path at
`ipc.rs:164-166`) and close the connection, so the handler completes promptly and the graceful drain
never blocks on it. No change to the wire protocol for well-behaved clients or to `dispatch`.

## Acceptance criteria
- A client that connects and sends nothing is dropped after the read deadline with a `bad_request`
  reply/close — it no longer pins the shutdown drain.
- A concurrent in-flight admin handler completes its commit AND writes its reply during a graceful
  shutdown (no abort of a committed-but-unacknowledged txn caused by an idle sibling).
- Well-behaved clients within the deadline are unaffected; wire protocol unchanged.
- Unit tests: an idle connection times out with `bad_request`; a normal request within the deadline
  still round-trips; graceful-shutdown test with one idle + one active connection drains cleanly.
- e2e / integration where warranted: extend the existing graceful-shutdown IPC test with an idle
  client, asserting the active handler's reply is observed (logged).
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean.

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No change to `MAX_REQUEST_BYTES`, the command surface, or the shutdown abort timeout itself; no
keep-alive / streaming. Just a read deadline on the single request frame.

## Coupled edits / dependencies
EXTENDS PR-15 (same `ipc.rs` connection path) — depends on / sequences after PR-15 to avoid
overlapping edits. Together they harden the IPC socket: PR-15 the bind→chmod window + peer-cred,
PR-24 the read deadline.
close_reason: Merged as PR #35 (squash): 2s read deadline on the single request frame (500ms in test), bounded best-effort bad_request reply on timeout, compile-time budget guard (READ_DEADLINE_PROD + REPLY_WRITE_BOUND < SHUTDOWN_DRAIN), warn log; idle client can no longer wedge the graceful drain into the abort window. Follow-up: lnrent-j3c (slow dispatch)

title: Buyer CLI --json contract on argv parse errors
description:
# PR-25 (HARDEN) — buyer CLI --json contract on argv parse errors

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-25 (2026-07-05 second pass); focused spec: none.

## Problem / evidence
The buyer CLI is the agent-facing surface of the ecosystem — a stranger's automation drives it with
`--json` and branches on the exit code. But argv parse failures escape the machine-readable contract:
```
clients/cli/src/main.rs:142-145
async fn main() -> ExitCode {
    let cli = Cli::parse();
    let json = cli.json;
```
`Cli::parse()` renders clap failures as plaintext usage on stderr and exits with clap's default
**2** — which both breaks the `{ok:false,error:{…}}` envelope AND collides with the taxonomy's exit
2 = `not_found`:
```
clients/core/src/error.rs:58-72
    /// … 0 ok; 2 not_found; 3 bad_request / invalid_state / unsupported_interactive; …
        BuyerError::NotFound(_) => 2,
        BuyerError::BadRequest(_) | … => 3,
```
So an agent that got exit 2 cannot tell "you passed bad flags" from "listing not found". (This is in
the `clients/cli` crate, not the daemon.)

## Design / fix
Minimal, in `clients/cli/src/main.rs`:
- Replace `Cli::parse()` with `Cli::try_parse()`.
- On the `Err(e)` arm, decide the surface by scanning the RAW argv for `--json` (the flag can't be
  read from the failed-to-parse `Cli`): if present, render a `bad_request` JSON envelope
  (`{"ok":false,"error":{"code":"bad_request","message":…,"retryable":false}}`) to stderr and exit
  **3** (matching `BuyerError::BadRequest`'s taxonomy code); if absent, fall back to clap's normal
  human usage/exit so `--help`/`--version` and interactive use are unchanged.
- Reuse the existing `render_err`/envelope shape so the error body matches every other `--json`
  failure the CLI already emits (`main.rs:432-444`).

## Acceptance criteria
- A bad-argv invocation WITH `--json` in argv emits a single `bad_request` JSON envelope on stderr,
  stdout stays clean, exit code is 3 (not 2, so no collision with `not_found`).
- A bad-argv invocation WITHOUT `--json` keeps clap's human usage output and default behavior.
- `--help` / `--version` still work on both paths.
- Unit tests: `try_parse` on a bad argv containing `--json` yields the exit-3 `bad_request` envelope;
  the same bad argv without `--json` takes the human path; a valid argv parses normally.
- e2e / integration where warranted: a CLI golden-path/`money_gate`-style assertion that a malformed
  `--json` invocation is a parseable envelope with exit 3.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, AND the clients/cli tests green
  (`cargo test -p lnrent-buyer` / the `clients/cli` test targets); `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean.

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No redesign of the CLI arg surface or the error taxonomy; no change to the daemon or buyer-core error
types; no colored/pretty JSON. Only the parse-error path. Do not touch the already-correct runtime
error rendering (`render_err`).

## Coupled edits / dependencies
None. Self-contained to the `clients/cli` crate (note it is NOT the daemon crate, so the extra
clients/cli test gate applies).
close_reason: Merged as PR #41 (squash): Cli::try_parse + handle_parse_error emits bad_request JSON envelope + exit 3 when --json in raw argv (fixing exit-2-collides-with-not_found); only explicit --help/--version bypass to exit 0; missing-subcommand routes to bad_request (codex P2); args_os scan is non-UTF-8-safe (codex P3)

title: Fedimint backend-index GC: fedimint_invoice/fedimint_pay grow unbounded under flood
description:
# Fedimint backend-index GC: `fedimint_invoice` / `fedimint_pay` grow unbounded under a flood

**Discovered during lnrent-y4m.2** (terminal-row GC reaper) via an adversarial money-safety review.

## Problem
The y4m.2 reaper bounds the daemon **store** DB, but the Fedimint backend keeps a **separate** index DB (`self.index`, a distinct `Mutex<Connection>` in `daemon/src/fedimint_backend.rs`). Every created invoice inserts a `fedimint_invoice` row (`idx_insert`, ~fedimint_backend.rs:1240-1246); on expiry it is only `UPDATE ... SET status='CANCELED'` (~:1279) — there is **no DELETE**. So on a Fedimint-backed deployment a distinct-`request_id` unpaid-order flood retains one dead `fedimint_invoice` row per request **forever**, re-exposing the exact disk-exhaustion vector y4m.2 closes for the store DB. `fedimint_pay` (~:239) likely has the same shape.

## Scope
- Reap terminal `fedimint_invoice` rows (status `CANCELED`, and `PAID` past a retention window once no longer needed for settlement lookup) + terminal `fedimint_pay` rows, in the backend-index DB.
- Mirror y4m.2 discipline: a retention const + clock-capped cutoff `< MIN(unixepoch(), now) - RETENTION`; NEVER reap a row still needed to answer `lookup_settlement` / an OPEN invoice; best-effort, off the reconcile tick or the backend's own maintenance.
- File-locked to `daemon/src/fedimint_backend.rs` (OUT of the y4m.2 store.rs lock — that is why this is a separate bead).

## Non-goals
No VACUUM, no config knob (a const suffices), no cross-DB coupling to the store reaper.
close_reason: Merged as PR #37 (squash): throttled best-effort GC of past-retention CANCELED fedimint_invoice rows (chunked delete, (status,expires_at) index, PAID never reaped) + recovery expiry-gate (receive_backfill_needed) so reaped rows aren't re-backfilled OPEN + zero-expiry GC/recovery agreement; fedimint_pay GC deferred to y4m.19

title: Ambiguous fedimint pay (UnexpectedError) marked FAILED + retried — double-pay risk
description:
# Ambiguous fedimint pay outcomes (`UnexpectedError`) are marked FAILED and retried — double-pay risk

**Discovered during lnrent-y4m.8** (gateway failover) via an adversarial money-safety review. **PRE-EXISTING** — not introduced by y4m.8 (the retry-on-FAILED logic predates it); y4m.8 only changed which gateway a retry uses.

## Problem
`await_pay` classifies EVERY non-success terminal fedimint `LnPayState` — including `LnPayState::UnexpectedError` — as `PayStatus::Failed` (`daemon/src/fedimint_backend.rs` ~:492). A later drive that sees `Failed` STARTS A NEW payment (~:578) and calls `pay_bolt11_invoice` again (~:676). But `UnexpectedError` is NOT proof the recipient was unpaid — it can be a POST-payment local failure — so retrying can send the money twice.

Mitigation today: Lightning's `payment_hash` bounds the actual loss — an invoice can only be claimed ONCE (one preimage), so a second attempt to the same invoice is rejected/refunded by the recipient/gateway. So a true double-CLAIM is unlikely, but the daemon can still lock funds twice transiently and its ledger/outlay accounting around a retried-ambiguous pay is not obviously correct.

## Scope
- Treat AMBIGUOUS terminal outcomes (`UnexpectedError`, and any state that is not a DEFINITIVE refund/cancellation) as PENDING/UNKNOWN, NOT `Failed`: re-await the SAME operation on the next drive rather than starting a new payment. Only retry after a DEFINITIVE refund/cancellation (funds provably returned).
- Distinguish `PayStatus::{Failed, Unknown/Pending}` at the `await_pay` classification site so the pay driver (`refund.rs`/`sweep.rs`) does not re-pay an unknown outcome.
- Money-core change — careful; NOT in y4m.8's selection-seam scope. File-locked to `daemon/src/fedimint_backend.rs` (+ possibly the `PayStatus` enum / the refund/sweep drivers that branch on it).

## Non-goals
No gateway-selection change (that is y4m.8). No change to the create-once invoice idempotency (already correct).
close_reason: Merged as PR #31 (squash): exhaustive pure PayStateClass classifiers; ambiguous terminals (UnexpectedError/RefundError) stay PENDING and re-await the same op (never a second pay); op-guarded CAS terminal marks; AwaitingChange->UnexpectedError upgrades to SUCCEEDED (preimage proven). Follow-ups filed: lnrent-kum P1 (recovery stale-FAILED double-pay), lnrent-7di P2 (SweepStuck alert), lnrent-mc8 P3 (persist preimage flag)

title: Config cannot CLEAR all fedimint gateway fallbacks (only update/preserve)
description:
# Config surface cannot CLEAR all fedimint gateway fallbacks

**Discovered during lnrent-y4m.8** (gateway failover) via a bot review (CodeRabbit).

## Problem
`sanitize_secret_string_list` collapses an explicit empty `fedimint_gateway_fallbacks = []` to `None` (empty == absent for source layering, `config.rs` ~:583). `resolve_durable_fedimint_config` then treats `None` as "omitted → preserve the durable list", so an operator CANNOT remove all fallbacks via config — a `[]` is indistinguishable from omitting the field. y4m.8 lets you ADD/UPDATE fallbacks (a non-empty list) and preserve-on-omit; the missing case is CLEAR-ALL. Workaround today: shorten to a non-empty list, or edit `fedimint.json` directly. A "removed" fallback gateway could otherwise still be used (INV-1 capped) as a fallback.

## Scope
Distinguish an EXPLICIT empty list (clear) from an OMITTED field (preserve) end-to-end: keep `Some([])` through sanitize/overlay for the fallbacks field (or a dedicated clear signal), and have `write_fedimint_config` apply an explicit empty list. File-locked to `daemon/src/config.rs`. Preserve: single-gateway byte-identical `fedimint.json`, the omit-preserves-durable behavior, the invite-conflict + corrupt-config fail-closed guards.

## Non-goals
No gateway-selection change (that is y4m.8).
close_reason: Implemented in y4m.8 PR #30: an explicit empty fedimint_gateway_fallbacks list now clears the durable list (sanitize preserves Some(vec![]) as an explicit-clear signal).

title: Fedimint refund fee-quote and pay can pick different gateways across a failover
description:
# Fedimint refund fee-quote and payment can select DIFFERENT gateways across a failover

**Discovered during lnrent-y4m.8** (gateway failover) via a bot review (CodeRabbit). MONEY-SAFE today (no loss), tracked for correctness.

## Problem
With ordered gateway failover (y4m.8), the refund fee QUOTE (`refund_net_sat` / `refund_required_outlay_msat`) and the actual PAY (`pay_inner` / `start_new_pay`) each call `select_gateway()` INDEPENDENTLY. If a failover happens BETWEEN the quote and the pay, gateway A prices the refund while gateway B pays it. A higher B fee can mark an otherwise-valid refund `FAILED` (it parks, then re-quotes against the current gateway on retry — self-healing); a lower B fee yields a slightly larger-than-quoted net to the buyer. NOT a loss: the pay is INV-1 capped against the ACTUAL pay gateway (no overpay), and a spuriously-parked refund is retried.

## Scope
Carry the SELECTED gateway (and its fees) through one refund attempt — quote and pay on ONE gateway decision — or re-quote against the pay gateway before recording FAILED. Heavy lift: thread the gateway identity through the refund flow (`refund.rs` + `fedimint_backend.rs` refund quote/pay). File-locked to `daemon/src/fedimint_backend.rs` (+ possibly `refund.rs`).

## Non-goals
No change to the INV-1 cap or the create-once idempotency (already correct).
close_reason: Folded into PR #30 (f85b87d): refund_quote returns net+gateway hint, pay_refund_capped_via prefers the quote-time gateway via pure ordered_with_preference with ordered fallback; hint never persisted; INV-1 cap unchanged; adversarial codex: no findings

title: Fedimint backend-index GC: reap terminal fedimint_pay rows (needs a created_at column)
description:
Follow-up carved out of lnrent-y4m.15 (which reaped terminal `fedimint_invoice` rows ONLY).

## Problem
The Fedimint backend index (`self.index` in daemon/src/fedimint_backend.rs) also holds a `fedimint_pay` table (outbound-refund idempotency, keyed by `idempotency_key`) and a `fedimint_pay_dead_op` ledger. y4m.15 deliberately did NOT reap these, for two reasons:
1. `fedimint_pay` has NO timestamp column, so there is no clock-capped retention cutoff to gate a safe delete on (the y4m.2/y4m.15 discipline needs `< MIN(unixepoch(), now) - RETENTION`).
2. A terminal (SUCCEEDED/FAILED) `fedimint_pay` row IS the idempotency record. Reaping it while its owning refund could still re-drive the same key would let `pay_inner` start a SECOND payment — a double-pay. Proving the owning refund is itself terminal is cross-DB (the store db, not the backend index), a non-goal for the flood-bounding beads.

`fedimint_pay` is also NOT a free-flood vector like `fedimint_invoice`: pays originate from refunds/sweeps, bounded by real economic activity, not by distinct-external_id unpaid-order spam. Hence P3.

## Scope
- Add a `created_at` column to `fedimint_pay` via the ALTER-with-dup-tolerance migration pattern (the same idempotent `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` that tolerates a re-run, as used for the downtime-credit column, lnrent-7fp.22).
- Reap terminal `fedimint_pay` rows (SUCCEEDED/FAILED) past a LONG retention safely beyond any crash-redrive / recover_pay_from_oplog window — much longer than the 30d invoice retention, because a stale-FAILED row still guards against the lnrent-kum double-pay until recovery has provably converged.
- Reap orphaned `fedimint_pay_dead_op` ledger rows only once their owning `fedimint_pay` row is gone (never strand a dead-op marker whose op could still be replayed).
- Mirror y4m.15: a retention const (no config knob), clock-capped cutoff, best-effort + throttled on the same create/pay path or a maintenance tick, file-locked to daemon/src/fedimint_backend.rs.

## Non-goals
No VACUUM. No cross-DB coupling to the store reaper. No change to the create-once / pay idempotency or the kum dead-op recovery semantics.
close_reason: done

comments.author: assistant
comments.text: Confirmed as the required y4m.15 follow-up: fedimint_pay GC stays out of y4m.15 because terminal rows are idempotency records; implementation needs created_at via duplicate-tolerant ALTER and a long crash-redrive-safe retention.

title: Terminal-row GC reaper (reservation/subscription/invoice/event_log)
description:
# PR-10 (HARDEN) — Terminal-row GC reaper for reservation/subscription/invoice/event_log

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-10.

## Problem / evidence
The only durable pruning today covers idempotency caches — nothing GCs the business tables:

```
daemon/src/store.rs:493  pub async fn prune_idempotency_caches(&self, now: i64) -> ...
daemon/src/store.rs:496      "DELETE FROM op_invocation ... finished_at < MIN(unixepoch(), ?1) - ?2"
daemon/src/store.rs:502      "DELETE FROM inbound_request ... created_at < MIN(unixepoch(), ?1) - ?2"
daemon/src/store.rs:22   pub const IDEMPOTENCY_CACHE_RETENTION_SECS: i64 = 120 * 24 * 60 * 60;
```

`seen_message` is the only other pruned table (90d):

```
daemon/src/nostr_engine.rs:58   const SEEN_MESSAGE_RETENTION_SECS: i64 = 90 * 24 * 60 * 60;
daemon/src/nostr_engine.rs:1145 "DELETE FROM seen_message WHERE seen_at < unixepoch() - ?1"
```

There is **no** `DELETE FROM reservation | subscription | invoice | event_log` anywhere.
Expiry/teardown only flip state (RELEASED / EXPIRED / TERMINATED / REFUNDED); the rows persist
forever. Since replay dedup stops only *duplicate* work, a flood of distinct `request_id`s
(the same vector PR-1/PR-2 bound) each mints fresh reservation/invoice/event_log rows — so the
DB grows without bound and eventually exhausts the operator's single-box disk. As of 2026-07-05
M6, `event_log` finally has scan indexes (`store.rs:297-300`) — but indexes bound scan cost,
not disk footprint; there is still no GC.

## Design / fix
Add a terminal-row reaper beside `prune_idempotency_caches`, on the same maintenance loop, with
an operator-tunable retention window. Delete only rows in **terminal** states past the cutoff:
reservation RELEASED/EXPIRED, subscription EXPIRED/TERMINATED/REFUNDED, terminal invoices, and
aged `event_log` rows. Reuse the existing clock-capped cutoff idiom
(`MIN(unixepoch(), now) - retention`) so synthetic-clock tests and a lagging daemon clock do not
over-prune. Delete in FK-safe order.

Invariant to preserve: `load_refund_readiness_liabilities` reconstructs liabilities from
`event_log` settle/refund kinds — the retention window MUST outlive any still-open refund
liability (or the event_log reap must exclude money-journal kinds while a liability references
them), so GC never erases the record of money owed. Only reap rows that are terminal AND
unreferenced.

## Acceptance criteria
- A reaper deletes terminal rows past the retention window; live / non-terminal rows are
  untouched; FK integrity holds after a reap.
- Unit tests: seed terminal and non-terminal rows straddling the cutoff; assert only
  terminal-past-window rows are deleted and the row sitting **exactly at** the cutoff behaves
  per the chosen (inclusive/exclusive) boundary — test that boundary explicitly. Assert an
  `event_log` row backing an open refund liability is NOT reaped.
- e2e / integration: a distinct-request-id flood followed by a reap tick returns per-table row
  counts to bounded; log deleted counts per table.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean

## Non-goals / scope discipline
Not a data-lifecycle framework: no VACUUM/auto-compaction, no per-table policy engine, no
archival/export before delete, no configurable retention beyond one window. One reaper + one
retention window beside the existing prune. Overengineering is the top project risk — keep it a
single DELETE-set, not a subsystem.

## Dependencies
- (compounds PR-1/PR-2's flood vector and PR-17's stale-cursor rescans, but no hard dependency)
close_reason: done

title: Disk-full / corruption handling on the money write path
description:
# PR-11 (HARDEN) — Disk-full / corruption handling on the money write path

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-11.

## Problem / evidence
Every money mutation runs through `Store::transaction`, which propagates any commit failure as
a bare error with no classification:

```
daemon/src/store.rs:448  pub async fn transaction<T, F>(&self, f: F) -> Result<T>
daemon/src/store.rs:454      let txn = conn.transaction()?;
daemon/src/store.rs:455      let out = f(&txn)?;
daemon/src/store.rs:456      txn.commit()?;
```

And `open()` runs only WAL + foreign_keys + migrate — no integrity check:

```
daemon/src/store.rs:411      conn.execute_batch("PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL; PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON;")?;
daemon/src/store.rs:412      migrate(&conn)?;
```

So an `ENOSPC` / `SQLITE_CORRUPT` / `SQLITE_IOERR` on a money commit surfaces as an
indistinguishable `Err`, there is no integrity-check-on-open, and there is no degraded mode. On
the operator's single box a full disk (which PR-10's unbounded growth makes reachable) or a
corrupt page fails money writes while the daemon keeps accepting orders and attempting captures
against a DB it cannot durably write — the worst posture for a stranger-operator holding real
funds.

## Design / fix
Two tightly-scoped additions, preserving the sole-writer actor + atomic-transaction invariants:

1. **Integrity-check-on-open:** run a `PRAGMA quick_check` in `open()` (cheaper than full
   `integrity_check`); a failure yields a clear structured startup error rather than a late
   opaque runtime failure.
2. **Defined degraded/read-only mode:** classify the `ENOSPC`/`SQLITE_CORRUPT`/`SQLITE_IOERR`
   family at the commit site; on such an error trip a degraded flag that refuses new money
   writes (orders/captures rejected with a defined error) while still serving reads / status /
   IPC, and fire a PR-5 alert once that sink lands. A mode flag + a guard on the write entry
   points — not a new subsystem.

## Acceptance criteria
- `open()` runs an integrity check; a corrupt/truncated DB yields a clear structured error, not
  a panic or a silent late failure.
- A classified disk-full/corrupt commit error trips degraded mode; subsequent money writes are
  refused with a defined error; reads/status still serve.
- Unit tests: inject a `SQLITE_IOERR`/`ENOSPC`-shaped rusqlite error at commit and assert
  degraded-mode entry + write refusal; a corrupt/truncated DB file on open is caught by
  `quick_check`. Boundary: a healthy DB passes `quick_check` and does NOT trip degraded mode.
- e2e / integration: back the DB on a tiny tmpfs (or simulate `ENOSPC`), drive a write to
  failure, and assert the daemon degrades rather than looping; log the degraded transition.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No auto-repair / `.recover`, no failover or secondary DB, no continuous background integrity
scanning, no online backup. Detect, refuse safely, alert — recovery stays the operator's manual
restore-from-backup path. Do not expand the money core; this is a guard around it.

## Dependencies
- (compounds PR-10 — a flood can fill the disk this must handle — but no hard dependency; the
  PR-5 alert sink is an enhancer, degrade-and-refuse works without it)
close_reason: done

title: Clear stale next_deadline cursor on REFUNDED / reset on PROVISIONING
description:
# PR-17 (HARDEN) — Clear stale next_deadline cursor on REFUNDED / reset on PROVISIONING

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-17.

## Problem / evidence
The reconcile cursor (`next_deadline`) is cleared to NULL on two terminal transitions:

```
daemon/src/reconcile.rs:717  "UPDATE subscription SET state='EXPIRED', next_deadline=NULL, updated_at=?2 ...
daemon/src/reconcile.rs:909  "UPDATE subscription SET state='TERMINATED', next_deadline=NULL, updated_at=?2 ...
```

But three other transitions never clear or reset it:

```
daemon/src/capture.rs:187    "UPDATE subscription SET state='PROVISIONING', updated_at=?2 WHERE id=?1"   (order capture)
daemon/src/provision.rs:884  "UPDATE subscription SET state='REFUND_DUE' ... WHERE ... state='PROVISIONING'"
daemon/src/refund.rs:793     "UPDATE subscription SET state='REFUNDED' ... WHERE ... state='REFUND_DUE'"
```

So a terminal `REFUNDED` row keeps `next_deadline <= now` and is re-selected on **every**
reconcile tick, only to fall into the totality no-op arm — unbounded per-tick rescans whose cost
grows with every accumulated terminal row (compounds PR-10's no-GC accumulation). This is NOT a
correctness bug: each transition is CAS-guarded, so re-selection just no-ops. It is purely a
scan-cost fix.

## Design / fix
Clear/reset the cursor at the three sites, mirroring what EXPIRED/TERMINATED already do, as a
one-line-per-site change to the existing UPDATE — preserving every CAS guard:

- `refund.rs:793` (terminal REFUNDED): add `next_deadline=NULL` to the CAS UPDATE.
- `capture.rs:187` (PROVISIONING): reset the cursor to the row's next real deadline (or NULL
  until provisioning sets one), so the stale order-invoice deadline stops re-selecting the row.
- `provision.rs:884` (REFUND_DUE): if the Refunder selects REFUND_DUE by **state** (not by
  deadline), clear the cursor; if it relies on the deadline as a live cursor, leave it. Verify
  which against the Refunder's selection query before changing this site.

## Acceptance criteria
- REFUNDED rows have `next_deadline IS NULL` and are no longer re-selected by the reconcile scan;
  PROVISIONING resets the cursor appropriately.
- Unit tests: drive a sub to REFUNDED and assert `next_deadline IS NULL`; assert a subsequent
  reconcile tick selects it **zero** times (the boundary — selected-terminal-row count == 0).
  Same assertion for the PROVISIONING reset.
- e2e / integration: accumulate N terminal REFUNDED rows and assert the per-tick selected-row
  count stays bounded (does not grow with N); log the per-tick scan/selection count.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean

## Non-goals / scope discipline
This is not a correctness change — do not touch the CAS guards or state totality. Do not
redesign the reconcile selection query into an index-only / partial-index scan (a separate perf
item). Just clear the cursor at the three named sites.

## Dependencies
- (compounds PR-10 — accumulated terminal rows are what the stale cursor rescans — but no hard
  dependency)
close_reason: done

title: Wrap each migration + user_version bump in one BEGIN..COMMIT
description:
# PR-18 (HARDEN) — Wrap each migration + user_version bump in one BEGIN..COMMIT

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-18.

## Problem / evidence
`migrate()` applies a migration and bumps `user_version` as **two separate** `execute_batch`
calls with no surrounding transaction:

```
daemon/src/store.rs:324  while (current as usize) < MIGRATIONS.len() {
daemon/src/store.rs:325      if let Err(e) = conn.execute_batch(MIGRATIONS[current as usize]) {
             ...
daemon/src/store.rs:343      conn.execute_batch(&format!("PRAGMA user_version = {}", current + 1))?;
daemon/src/store.rs:344      current += 1;
```

Today's shipped migrations are individually near-atomic and there is bespoke self-heal for the
two known duplicate-column cases (M3 `suspend_not_before`, M4 refund-resolver columns). But a
future **multi-statement** migration could half-apply: a crash between two of its statements, or
between the migration batch and the `user_version` bump, leaves the schema partially migrated or
re-runs the whole batch on restart (some statements then duplicate-fail). For a stranger-operator
upgrading the binary against a live money DB, that is schema corruption on the durability path.

## Design / fix
Wrap each `MIGRATIONS[i]` batch **and** its `user_version` bump in one `BEGIN..COMMIT` (SQLite
DDL is transactional; `PRAGMA user_version` is a writable header field inside a transaction).
On any error, roll back so the migration either fully applies + bumps, or does neither —
`user_version` never advances past a partially-applied migration, and a mid-migration crash
leaves a clean pre-migration DB that re-applies fresh on restart. Keep the existing M3/M4
duplicate-column self-heal (it handles the fresh-DB-vs-legacy-DB shape) working within the new
transaction boundary. Preserve idempotency: opening a current DB stays a no-op.

## Acceptance criteria
- Each migration + its `user_version` bump commits atomically; a failure mid-migration leaves
  `user_version` unadvanced and the schema unchanged (no partial DDL).
- Unit tests: a synthetic multi-statement migration that fails on its **second** statement
  leaves `user_version` at the pre-migration value and applies none of its DDL (the boundary —
  full rollback of the failing migration). The existing fresh-DB path and the legacy-v2/v3
  self-heal paths still pass.
- e2e / integration: simulate a crash (drop the connection) between migration and bump, reopen,
  and assert the migration re-applies cleanly (no leading-duplicate failure) and reaches
  `SCHEMA_VERSION`; log the applied-versions sequence.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean

## Non-goals / scope discipline
Do not rewrite the migration framework or the ordered-array model; no down-migrations/rollback
DDL; do not remove the M3/M4 self-heal branches. Add only the transaction boundary around each
existing step.

## Dependencies
-
close_reason: Merged as PR #40 (squash): each migration batch + user_version bump wrapped in one BEGIN IMMEDIATE..COMMIT (apply_migrations), rolled back on error so a half-applied migration can't corrupt the money DB; M3/M4 self-heal preserved; synthetic-failing-migration test proves atomicity + that user_version is transactional

title: Optional passphrase-encrypted backup mode
description:
# PR-20 (HARDEN) — Optional passphrase-encrypted backup mode

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-20.

## Problem / evidence
`backup()` writes the operator's fund-controlling secrets in plaintext (hardened 0600/0700,
symlink-refused, but unencrypted):

```
daemon/src/backup.rs:111  pub fn backup(data_dir: &Path, dest: &Path) -> Result<Manifest> {
daemon/src/backup.rs:318      conn.execute("VACUUM INTO ?1", [dest_str])   // ecash-bearing state DB
daemon/src/backup.rs:327      fs::copy(src, dst)                           // operator.seed + fedimint.json
daemon/src/backup.rs:341  fn copy_dir_recursive(src: &Path, dst: &Path)    // fedimint client.db (ecash)
```

The captured set is the BIP39 seed (`operator.seed`, controls the identity **and all ecash
funds**), `fedimint.json`, and the ecash-bearing fedimint `client.db/` plus the state DB. That
is fine for a cold, operator-controlled data dir — but a backup's whole purpose is portability,
and the moment a stranger-operator rsyncs it to a USB stick or a cloud bucket it becomes a
fund-controlling seed at rest that anyone who obtains the media can sweep. In an ecosystem of
independent operators, plaintext-only is a foot-gun.

## Design / fix
Offer an **optional** passphrase-encrypted mode via a flag / passphrase argument on the backup
CLI subcommand. When a passphrase is supplied, encrypt the artifact with a well-reviewed AEAD
(prefer an existing audited crate — e.g. `age`, or XChaCha20-Poly1305 with an Argon2id-derived
key — over hand-rolled crypto) and record `encrypted: true` in `MANIFEST.json` so `restore`
knows to require the passphrase. `restore` gains the symmetric decrypt path.

Keep the existing plaintext cold-dir path as the **default** — no passphrase means today's
behavior, byte-for-byte unchanged. Encrypt the whole artifact (tar-then-encrypt, or per-file
encryption) — whichever is simplest across seed + fedimint dir + state DB. Passphrase-derived
symmetric key only; invent no key-management scheme.

## Acceptance criteria
- `backup` with a passphrase produces an encrypted artifact; without one, produces today's
  plaintext backup unchanged.
- `restore` reads `MANIFEST.json`, requires the passphrase, decrypts, and yields a
  byte-identical data dir.
- Unit / round-trip tests: encrypt-then-decrypt round-trips seed + fedimint dir + state DB to
  identical bytes; a wrong passphrase fails cleanly on the auth tag with **no** partial-write of
  a corrupt data dir; the no-passphrase plaintext path still round-trips. Boundary: empty vs
  present passphrase selects the mode.
- e2e / integration: an offline backup -> encrypt -> restore proof mirroring the existing
  offline backup test; log the mode chosen (plaintext vs encrypted).
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No key files / hardware keys / multi-recipient; no cloud upload or scheduling; no encryption of
the LIVE data dir at rest; no re-encryption / key-rotation. One optional passphrase mode beside
the existing plaintext path — not a KMS.

## Dependencies
-
close_reason: done

title: env-clear + allowlist hook spawns; scrub seed from daemon env
description:
# PR-12 (HARDEN) — env-clear + allowlist hook spawns; scrub seed from daemon env

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-12; focused spec: docs/specs/hook-env-hygiene.md.

> **Tier note:** tiered HARDEN, but per the 2026-07-04 grill this **GATES GO-LIVE** by operator
> decision — it sits on the critical path (GATE-0 → PR-5 → **PR-12** → 7qc dogfood → go-live).
> The documented runbook never leaks the seed to hooks (bootstrap is one-shot and hookless; run
> reads the seed from disk), but the operator wants the misuse path closed **in code, not prose**
> before real money touches the box. Prioritized P1 for that reason.

## Problem / evidence
lnrent seeds an ecosystem of independent third-party operators — the operator is a stranger who
may (against advice) supply the seed via the environment, exactly the path the daemon recommends.

`spawn_hook` builds the child with no `.env_clear()`, so every provision/suspend/resume/destroy/op
hook inherits the daemon's full environment:
```
daemon/src/runner.rs:104-110
    match Command::new(hook)
        .stdin(Stdio::piped())
        .stdout(Stdio::piped())
        .stderr(Stdio::piped())
        .kill_on_drop(true) // backstop; we also reap explicitly
        .spawn()
```
The recommended seed-supply path is the environment — the bootstrap warning steers operators there
(`daemon/src/main.rs:356-359`: "prefer LNRENT_MNEMONIC…"), `config.rs:457` reads env into config,
and nothing ever `remove_var`s it. So the BIP39 master seed (controls the operator identity AND all
ecash) sits in the daemon env for its lifetime and is copied into every hook process — exposed to
any hook `set -x`, an env-dumping tool failure, a crash core, or `/proc/<hook-pid>/environ`. This
violates SPEC §13 ("secrets ride stdin JSON, not argv or env"). `LNRENT_FEDIMINT_INVITE` /
`LNRENT_FEDIMINT_GATEWAY` ride along (lower severity, same class). Secrets already reach hooks
legitimately via stdin JSON (`op_dispatch.rs:191-208`), so nothing legitimate breaks.

## Design / fix
Follow docs/specs/hook-env-hygiene.md as source of truth. Three parts:

**A. Scrub bootstrap secrets from the daemon's own env.** After `raw_config_from_env` is consumed
(both bootstrap and run paths), `std::env::remove_var` each `LNRENT_MNEMONIC` /
`LNRENT_FEDIMINT_INVITE` / `LNRENT_FEDIMINT_GATEWAY`. Because `remove_var` is `unsafe`/thread-unsafe
in newer Rust and `#[tokio::main]` builds the runtime before any `main`-body code runs, replace
`#[tokio::main]` with a synchronous `fn main()` that reads env into config/`Zeroizing`, `remove_var`s
while still single-threaded, THEN builds the runtime explicitly and `block_on`s the async entrypoint.
This is defense-in-depth (does NOT overwrite the kernel-placed initial env block, so the daemon's own
`/proc/<pid>/environ` may still show it — document the caveat + recommend systemd-credential/stdin).

**B. `.env_clear()` + allowlist in `spawn_hook` (the load-bearing fix).** `spawn_hook` is the single
choke point (provision/reconcile/resume/op_dispatch all reach it via `run_hook`). `.env_clear()`,
then pass through only the base `PATH, HOME, LANG, LC_ALL, TZ, TMPDIR`. `LNRENT_*` never passes by
construction. **Recipe-declared passthrough:** the recipe manifest gains optional
`provisioning.env = ["…"]`; `Recipe::validate` bounds it (≤16 entries, each `[A-Z0-9_]{1,64}`, and
REJECTS any name starting with `LNRENT`). Absent listed vars skip silently (the hook fails with its
own clearer missing-token error).

**THE ONE DOCUMENTED EXCEPTION — DO_TOKEN.** go-live.md §4 runs the daemon with `DO_TOKEN=<token>`
in its env (`docs/go-live.md:80`) and every do-vps hook reads `$DO_TOKEN`
(`recipes/do-vps/provision:17,26`, plus suspend/resume/destroy/healthcheck/ops). So the allowlist
MUST admit it via the recipe's declared env-passthrough list. Ship `recipes/do-vps/recipe.toml` with
`env = ["DO_TOKEN", "DO_REGION", "DO_SIZE", "DO_IMAGE"]` — the hooks read all four
(`recipes/do-vps/provision:17-20`; `DO_REGION/DO_SIZE/DO_IMAGE` are optional operator overrides with
baked defaults), so a `DO_TOKEN`-only list would silently drop those knobs. `recipes/dummy` and
`wireguard` declare nothing.

## Acceptance criteria
- No `LNRENT_*` var reaches any hook even when the daemon started with `LNRENT_MNEMONIC` set; base
  vars present; a recipe-declared var (`DO_TOKEN`) passes through when set in the daemon env; an
  undeclared var does not.
- After startup, in-process `std::env::var("LNRENT_MNEMONIC")` returns `Err`. (Do NOT assert on
  `/proc/self/environ` — `remove_var` cannot overwrite the kernel-placed block.)
- `Recipe::validate` rejects `env = ["LNRENT_MNEMONIC"]`, oversize lists (>16), and malformed names.
- Unit tests: env-dumping test hook proving the leak then the fix; base-var-present + declared-var +
  undeclared-var passthrough cases; `Recipe::validate` reject cases; post-startup `env::var` == Err.
- e2e / integration where warranted: do-vps provisioning still works with `DO_TOKEN` in the daemon
  env (existing live/e2e unchanged); dummy-recipe e2e (needs no env) stays green; existing runner
  tests (timeout, output cap, reap) stay green — log the env-construction path under the leak test.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean.

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No secret-manager integration; no per-op env lists (recipe-level is enough); no change to the
stdin-JSON hook input contract or hook I/O; no attempt to scrub `/proc/self/environ` history beyond
`remove_var`; no changes for the config-file/stdin seed paths (already safe). Keep existing
zeroization exactly as is.

## Coupled edits / dependencies
go-live.md §4 must be RE-VERIFIED in the SAME change: add one line noting hooks receive ONLY the base
env + the recipe's declared `env` list (so `DO_TOKEN` flows because do-vps declares it), and document
the `/proc/self/environ` caveat + systemd-credential/stdin recommendation. Also touch SPEC §7.2 (hook
child-env contract) and SPEC §13 (the "secrets via stdin, not env" claim becomes fully true — note
the enforcement point). No bead dependencies.
close_reason: Merged as PR #16 (0b979bf): env-clear+allowlist in spawn_hook (threaded through 5 callers), recipe provisioning.env with LNRENT-reject validation, do-vps declares DO_* vars, sync-main seed scrub; codex xhigh review PASS (0 findings); 470 tests + clippy (native+wasm) + mock build green

title: Gateway failover / hot-swap (single pinned gateway fails closed)
description:
# PR-13 (HARDEN) — gateway failover / hot-swap (single pinned gateway fails closed)

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-13; focused spec: none.

## Problem / evidence
An ecosystem operator is a stranger running unattended: when their one configured Lightning gateway
goes down, both refunds AND receiving stop, and the only remedy today is edit-config + restart.

Config carries exactly one gateway pubkey:
```
daemon/src/config.rs:86-88
pub struct FedimintConfig {
    pub invite: String,
    pub gateway: String,
}
```
The backend pins that single gateway and fails closed when it is unavailable — invoice creation
requires a gateway, so this blocks receiving as well as refunds:
```
daemon/src/fedimint_backend.rs:653-659
        // A gateway is REQUIRED for an externally-payable invoice (codex finding #1). get_gateway
        // refreshes the cache then picks one; Err if the federation has none registered.
        let gateway = ln
            .get_gateway(self.gateway, false)
            .await
            .context("selecting the configured lightning gateway")?
            .context("the configured (or any) lightning gateway is unavailable")?;
```

## Design / fix
Minimal, preserving the money invariants (idempotent create-on-external_id, ledger-authoritative
money). Either:
- Config gains a gateway **list** (ordered); the backend tries them in order and uses the first
  reachable one for invoice-create and refund-pay. Preserve the `create_lock` + external_id
  idempotency so failover between attempts never mints a second gateway invoice for one external_id.
- OR a hot-swap IPC command + `lnrent` subcommand that atomically swaps the active gateway without a
  restart, serialized through the same store/backend actor.

Pick the list-with-failover as the default (no operator action needed mid-outage); the hot-swap verb
is the smaller alternative if the list proves too invasive. Document the inherent limit: the
receive-side single-point-of-failure is intrinsic to the LN-invoice receive model (there is no
ecash-native receive path), so failover reduces but cannot eliminate gateway dependence.

## Acceptance criteria
- With a healthy gateway later in the list (or after a hot-swap), invoice-create and refund-pay
  succeed even though the first-listed gateway is down.
- Money invariants intact: no double-mint across a failover for one external_id; refunds stay
  INV-1 fee-capped and single-ledgered.
- Unit tests: failover picks the next reachable gateway; single-gateway config still works
  (back-compat); idempotent create under a mid-attempt gateway switch returns the stored invoice.
- e2e / integration where warranted: a regtest/mock scenario where the primary gateway is
  unreachable and receiving/refund proceed via the fallback, with the failover logged.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean.

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No multi-federation support; no automatic gateway discovery/reputation; no change to the money core
beyond the gateway-selection seam. Document — do not attempt to engineer away — the inherent
receive-side single-point-of-failure. Single-box operator scope unchanged.

## Coupled edits / dependencies
None. Independent of PR-14's reachability probe (though a preflight gateway check complements it).
close_reason: Merged as PR #30 (squash): ordered gateway+gateway_fallbacks failover, pure select_first_reachable seam, all 5 get_gateway sites routed, fails closed; adversarial pass caught+fixed stale-cache failover + corrupt-config clobber

title: lnrent preflight/doctor with real external reachability checks
description:
# PR-14 (HARDEN) — lnrent preflight/doctor with real external reachability checks

**Roadmap:** production-readiness.md PR-14; focused spec: none (SPEC already describes `lnrent doctor`).

## Problem / evidence
The operability surface is the product a stranger-operator onboards through. Today bootstrap
validation is strong on durable-state integrity but pings **nothing external**: a well-formed-but-
wrong gateway or federation invite passes validation and only fails at runtime `join_or_open`, and
`DO_TOKEN` validity is never checked at startup. go-live.md §4 papers over this with manual steps —
`lnrent money` for `Gateway: ok`/`READY` (`docs/go-live.md:91`) and a hand-run
`curl … api.digitalocean.com/v2/account` for the token (`docs/go-live.md:92`) — which a stranger can
skip or get wrong. SPEC already describes `lnrent doctor`; it is unimplemented.

## Design / fix
Add a `lnrent preflight` (a.k.a. `doctor`) IPC command + subcommand that actually PROBES the three
external dependencies and reports each pass/fail with a diagnostic, not just durable-state validity:
- **Gateway reachability** — reuse the existing gateway-selection path (the `get_gateway` seam,
  `fedimint_backend.rs:653-659`) to confirm a usable gateway is registered/reachable.
- **Federation reachability** — an explicit federation-liveness probe distinct from
  gateway/balance (this is the same probe PR-9 wants for the readiness report; share it). A
  well-formed-but-wrong invite must FAIL here, not silently at first `join_or_open`.
- **Provider token** — validate `DO_TOKEN` at startup/preflight (the check go-live does by hand):
  a lightweight authenticated call the do-vps recipe's `env`-declared token can make.
Report structured per-check results; non-zero exit when any check fails so an agent/operator can
gate announce on it. Keep it a thin prober, not a monitoring framework.

## Acceptance criteria
- `lnrent preflight`/`doctor` returns per-check pass/fail for gateway + federation + provider token,
  with a machine-readable summary and a non-zero exit on any failure.
- A well-formed-but-unreachable gateway or wrong federation invite is caught by preflight, NOT
  deferred to runtime `join_or_open`.
- An invalid/absent `DO_TOKEN` is reported at preflight rather than surfacing only on first provision.
- Unit tests: each check's pass and fail branch (mock the external probes); aggregate exit code.
- e2e / integration where warranted: a scenario with a deliberately-bad gateway/invite and a bad
  token, asserting preflight fails each with a distinct diagnostic (logged).
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `cargo clippy --all-targets` clean.

## Non-goals / scope discipline
No continuous health monitoring (that overlaps PR-5 alerting / PR-9 status — this is a one-shot
prober); no auto-remediation; no new external service dependencies. Reuse the existing
gateway/federation seams rather than building parallel clients. Keep it tightly scoped to the three
named reachability checks.

## Coupled edits / dependencies
Shares the federation-liveness probe with PR-9 (build once, use in both preflight and the readiness
report) — coordinate but not a hard dependency. Update go-live.md §4 to call `lnrent preflight`
instead of the manual `lnrent money` + `curl` steps once landed.
close_reason: Merged as PR #33 (squash): lnrent preflight/doctor probing gateway (refund_gateway_ready) + federation (backend_ready) + DO token (injectable probe, redirects refused, distinct absent/malformed/rejected/unreachable diagnostics); structural agent-grade exit gating; serialized reports; 18 tests

title: Buyer cancel/renew during the transient RESUMING state
description:
**RESUMING renew/cancel is P3 UX-ONLY — the money bug CANNOT occur.** The feared cancel/renew-during-RESUMING money-loss path does not exist: order_intake gates cancel and renew to ACTIVE|SUSPENDED (order_intake.rs:317,379,922), so a cancel/renew that lands in the transient RESUMING window is simply DROPPED (Ok(()) + warn) — it never flips state, never bypasses the resume driver, never strands a paid renewal. The money-path audit confirmed this (production-readiness.md "Dispositions for existing open beads", lnrent-z4u: downgrade to a P3 UX residue).

## Residual — the only work here
The buyer gets NO reply DM when they cancel/renew during the transient RESUMING window (usually sub-second; up to minutes on a slow/failing resume hook, 3x120s). RESUMING self-resolves (resume -> ACTIVE, or fail -> SUSPENDED+refund), after which cancel/renew behave normally. This is a small operator/buyer UX polish, not a correctness fix.

## Scope
- Decide and implement ONE of: (a) send an informational "a renewal is being applied — please retry in a moment" reply for a cancel/renew received in RESUMING, or (b) keep the drop and DOCUMENT it as accepted behavior. Note renew has no error-reply variant in wire::Msg, so a renew reply would ride billing.notice or accept the drop — settle that in the design.
- NO state machinery: do NOT add a RESUMING->CANCELLED transition, a pending-cancel queue, resume-driver integration, or any refund/restore path. Those are OUT of scope (they would be a new feature guarding a bug that cannot occur — overengineering, the top project risk).

## Guardrail — preserve, for a FUTURE scope expansion ONLY (not this bead)
If a later effort ever expands scope to make cancel actually FUNCTION during RESUMING: do NOT naively flip RESUMING->CANCELLED — that bypasses the resume driver (which CASes on state='RESUMING'), leaving the renewal neither resumed nor refunded (paid, VM off, no refund; this was a reverted codex-gate P1). The safe design would route the cancel THROUGH the resume driver (record a pending-cancel applied after it resolves, or have the resume path claim RESUMING-origin CANCELLED rows and refund/restore). Recorded so the trap is never re-discovered the hard way — explicitly NOT in scope here.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A short design note decides reply-vs-documented-drop for cancel/renew in RESUMING; if a reply is chosen it is a plain informational DM with NO state change.
- No RESUMING->CANCELLED shortcut and no new refund/resume/pending-cancel machinery is introduced (grep/review check).
- Reply path: a unit test that a cancel/renew received while the sub is RESUMING produces the informational reply and leaves sub state unchanged. Documented-drop path: the behavior is documented and a test asserts the drop leaves state unchanged.
- Gates: `cargo test -p lnrentd` green, `clippy` clean.

Related: [[PR-21]] (lnrent-urw.8) — the other RESUMING-window correctness concern (suspend/destroy hook race). This bead is UX-only; PR-21 is the money-core one.

title: Supervisor catch-up must honor a live settled_at (caps a late LIVE payment -> wrong provision)
description:
## Problem (codex P1; gated behind fedimint-off but also latent on the mock path)
supervisor.rs settlement_catch_up (boot + maintenance loop) scans OPEN store invoices and for any the backend reports lookup()==Paid FABRICATES a CAPPED recovered_settled_at (supervisor.rs:~1033/1049: capped < expires_at for an order). Correct for a RECOVERY settlement (settled-while-down, true time unknown) but WRONG for a LIVE settlement whose true settled_at is known: a late LIVE payment that should REFUND (capture g5p gate at capture.rs:180 refunds at settled_at>=expires_at) can be grabbed by catch-up first -> stamped expires_at-1 -> PROVISIONED. capture is idempotent on external_id so whoever captures first wins -> non-deterministic.

## Fix (design corrected by the codex next-steps review 2026-06-29)
catch-up must use the backend's REAL observed settled_at when known, and CAP only on recovery provenance (true time unknown).
- TRAIT: make lookup() richer OR add `lookup_settlement(id) -> {status, settled_at: Option<i64>}`. catch-up uses Some(real) EXACTLY and caps only on None.
- FedimintPayment: ALREADY persists settled_at in its index (fedimint_backend.rs:74/720) but its lookup() DISCARDS it (fedimint_backend.rs:357) — surface it.
- MockPayment: does NOT currently store settlement time — it keeps only `paid: HashSet<String>` and DROPS settled_at from settle() (backends.rs:187/219). CHANGE the mock to store external_id->settled_at (a live mock settle records the real ts; a recovery has None). [the earlier bead wrongly said the mock already stored it]
- capture: NO policy change needed (g5p gate already keys off the real Settlement.settled_at).
- TEST: a regression proving a LATE live-paid order REFUNDS (real ts >= expiry) while a RECOVERED/NULL paid order still uses the conservative cap (provisions).

## Notes
Latent on mock today (the .15 e2e tests are deterministic) but a real production race; fix BEFORE enabling fedimint (it is o6p's only graph blocker besides the resolver, now done). Files: supervisor.rs, backends.rs (trait + MockPayment), fedimint_backend.rs (lookup), a new catch-up test.
close_reason: DONE + merged (master 490c4ac). Supervisor settlement_catch_up now honors a LIVE settled_at: added PaymentBackend::lookup_settlement(id)->(PaymentStatus, Option<i64>); catch-up uses Some(real) exactly + caps (recovered_settled_at) only on None. MockPayment stores external_id->settled_at + settle_recovered() models recovery; FedimintPayment::lookup_settlement reads its index settled_at (Some=live Claimed, NULL=cached/recovery); lookup() unchanged (reconcile untouched); NO capture policy change. Regression settlement_catch_up_refunds_late_live_order_but_caps_recovery + existing recovery scenarios switched to settle_recovered (cap preserved). Built via watched rb-lite — converged CLEAN in 1 codex+claude round; cargo test + clippy (default + --features fedimint) green.

title: lnv2 backup/restore->reopen->spend has no repeatable test (deleted with lnv1's live test)
description:
No repeatable test proves an lnv2 backup can be RESTORED, REOPENED, and SPENT. Both local reviewers of the lnv1-deletion PR (#51) flagged this — codex ranked it P1, fable P3; the truth is in between, so P1.

## What exists vs what's missing
- `daemon/tests/backup.rs` (offline) proves the backup/restore MECHANISM: it copies the fedimint subtree + state DB as opaque bytes and rereads them. It is deliberately schema-agnostic (its own comment: "the exact table shape is immaterial") and, as of PR #51, seeds the real `lnv2_index.db`/`lnv2_invoice`/`lnv2_pay` names. Good, but it never constructs an `Lnv2Payment` on the restored dir.
- The deleted `daemon/tests/fedimint_live.rs` was the only test that did cold backup -> restore -> REOPEN the backend -> verify a prior pay -> SPEND restored ecash. It exercised LNV1. There is no lnv2 equivalent.
- lnrent-7fp.14 (backup PART A) built the offline round-trip; lnrent-7qc proved lnv2 end to end but did NOT test backup/restore, and was a manual dogfood, not an artifact.

## Why it matters (bounded honestly)
The spendable ecash is in the fedimint RocksDB, copied opaquely and byte-round-trip-verified, so the acute "money stranded" risk is low. The genuine untested claim is that `Lnv2Payment::join_or_open` can OPEN a restored data dir and resume the money path (spend, and correlate pending pays/refunds via the restored `lnv2_index.db`). `daemon/src/identity.rs:41` asserts lnv2 is "backup/restore proven"; that proof was lnv1's and is now gone.

## Work
- Port the cold backup -> restore -> reopen -> verify-prior-pay -> spend flow to `Lnv2Payment` as a `#[ignore]`d live test (mirror `lnv2_live.rs`'s harness; needs a live/devimint federation).
- OR, if a fully live test is too heavy, at minimum an offline test that constructs `Lnv2Payment::join_or_open` against a restored data dir and asserts it opens + reads back a seeded `lnv2_index.db` row (closes the "reopen" gap without a federation).
- Correct `identity.rs:41` if the claim outruns the coverage.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A repeatable test exercises lnv2 restore -> reopen -> (spend or at least resume), not just the opaque byte round-trip.
- `identity.rs:41`'s "backup/restore proven" claim is backed by an lnv2 artifact or softened.
close_reason: lnv2 cold backup/restore->reopen->spend live test added (PR #52, merged 2026-07-22) and PROVEN live against mainnet solomint (46.58s): prior pay status survived restore + restored ecash spendable. Ported from the deleted lnv1 fedimint_backup_restore_preserves_ecash_live; re-backs the identity.rs:41 'backup/restore proven live' claim for lnv2.

title: gateway config field is required at bootstrap but ignored by the lnv2 backend
description:
`FedimintConfig.gateway` is a REQUIRED bootstrap field that the live lnv2 backend never reads. For a stranger operator (operability is the product surface), this is a real wart: onboarding (docs/go-live.md §1, §3) tells them to obtain "the gateway's pubkey" and set `LNRENT_FEDIMINT_GATEWAY=<pubkey>`, bootstrap ENFORCES the invite+gateway pairing (config.rs), and then the value is ignored — lnv2 selects its gateway by API SafeUrl (`lnv2_backend.rs` / `main.rs:494` "not consulted here").

Found by both local reviewers of PR #51 (fable P2, codex "pre-existing"). It pre-dates lnv1 deletion (lnv2 has ignored the gateway since lnrent-3d5/o6p); PR #51 only made the surrounding doc comments accurate (config.rs now says the field is unconsumed) without changing behavior — deliberately out of a deletion's scope.

The lnv1 code that DID consume `gateway` + `gateway_fallbacks` (the y4m.8 `[gateway] ++ fallbacks` failover selection) was deleted with lnv1.

## Options (pick in this bead)
1. DROP the fields: remove `gateway`/`gateway_fallbacks` from FedimintConfig + bootstrap validation + go-live.md, so operators aren't asked for an ignored value. Simplest; loses a future hook.
2. WIRE them into lnv2's native selection: lnv2 selects by SafeUrl, so a pubkey field is the wrong shape — this would mean an operator-configurable gateway-URL preference/allowlist feeding `reachable_gateway_preferring`. More work; gives operators real gateway control.

Lean = (1) unless there's product appetite for operator gateway pinning on lnv2.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A stranger operator is not asked at bootstrap for a value the daemon ignores.
- go-live.md no longer instructs setting an inert gateway pubkey (or it becomes a real, consumed setting).
- Whichever option: config parsing, bootstrap validation, docs, and any tests are consistent.

title: lnv2 send fee gate is lexicographic (base,ppm): low-base/high-ppm gateway bypasses SEND_FEE_LIMIT
description:
lnv2 `send()`'s fee gate compares `PaymentFee` LEXICOGRAPHICALLY (base, then ppm), so it is not a tight ~1.5%+100sat ceiling: a schedule like `99_999 msat base + arbitrarily high ppm` sorts BELOW `SEND_FEE_LIMIT` (100_000 msat, 15_000 ppm) on the base field and is ACCEPTED, then charges a proportional fee far above 1.5%. Surfaced by the codex reviewer on PR #50 (ADR-0019), confirmed against the pinned fedimint source (`modules/fedimint-lnv2-common/src/gateway_api.rs:200-203`: `struct PaymentFee { base: Amount, parts_per_million: u64 }` with a derived lexicographic Ord) and lnrent's own mirror (`daemon/src/lnv2_backend.rs:382-397` `lnv2_send_usable`: `(base, ppm) <= (LIMIT_BASE, LIMIT_PPM)`).

## Why lnrent is MOSTLY protected, and the residual
lnrent's INV-1 preflight cap is NOT the lexicographic gate — it is `lnv2_worst_fee_msat(fee, pay_msat)` (`lnv2_backend.rs:358`), which prices the ACTUAL advertised schedule against the actual payout and enforces `payout + worst_fee <= gross`. So a high-ppm gateway visible at preflight is correctly rejected (its worst_fee blows the cap). `lnv2_send_usable` only mirrors send()'s gate for gateway SELECTION (skip a gateway send() would refuse), not for the cap.

The RESIDUAL is a re-price TOCTOU: a gateway that shows an in-cap schedule at our preflight, then RE-PRICES to a low-base/high-ppm schedule before the actual `send()`. `send()` re-fetches routing info and accepts the new schedule via its lexicographic gate (which the new schedule passes), and debits the high fee — exceeding our reserved cap for that one refund. It is bounded only by the lexicographic gate, i.e. effectively unbounded in ppm for a sub-100-sat base. This is documented in ADR-0019 as the "narrow re-price TOCTOU" residual on INV-1.

## Options
1. COMPONENTWISE guard before/around send: reject any gateway whose ppm alone exceeds the SEND_FEE_LIMIT ppm (and base alone exceeds the base), rather than the lexicographic tuple compare — closes the low-base/high-ppm hole in lnrent's own selection AND re-check. Does not fix send()'s own gate (upstream) but means lnrent never SELECTS such a gateway and can re-validate the actual fee post-send.
2. POST-SEND assert: after send returns, check the actual routingFee against the reserved cap; alert/park on overrun (detector, not preventer — the money already left, same weakness as the rejected phoenixd reserve scheme).
3. UPSTREAM: fix `PaymentFee`'s Ord (or `send()`'s check) to be componentwise, in the douglaz/fedimint fork. Cleanest, but changes the fork's behavior vs upstream.
4. ACCEPT + document: the attack requires a guardian-vetted gateway to maliciously re-price in a sub-second window for one refund's fee; may be below the risk bar. Currently option 4 (documented in ADR-0019).

Lean: (1) — a componentwise selection guard is cheap, purely additive, and closes the hole on lnrent's side; combine with (2)'s post-send assert for defense in depth. Decide here.

## Acceptance Criteria
- lnrent no longer selects (or, post-send, silently accepts) a gateway whose per-component fee exceeds SEND_FEE_LIMIT, closing the low-base/high-ppm lexicographic hole on lnrent's side.
- A unit test constructs a `99_999 msat base + huge ppm` GatewaySendFee and asserts lnrent rejects it (today `lnv2_send_usable` ACCEPTS it — regression anchor).
- ADR-0019's "narrow re-price TOCTOU" note updated to reflect whatever guard lands (or explicitly reaffirms acceptance).

comments.author: master
comments.text:
RELATED RESIDUAL (from the PR #50 review, ADR-0019 residual #2): distinct from the lexicographic
fee-gate hole this bead tracks, there is a SMALL note-set residual on the INV-1 cap. `pay_inner`'s
`pay_start_lock` serializes OUTBOUND pays (no refund/sweep can interleave a send's dry-run→send
window), but it does NOT serialize INBOUND claims. A concurrent incoming receive (settling ecash)
that changes the wallet note set between `outgoing_outlay_msat`'s dry-run and the actual `send()` can
make the finalizer pick different mint inputs/change, and mint consensus fees are NON-MONOTONE in
note selection (`net_payout_sat` comment), so the finalized debit can slightly exceed the priced cap
even with a stable gateway schedule. Bounded small (a few consensus-fee units), unlike the fee-gate
hole. Whether to close it (e.g. re-check the outlay post-selection, or hold a broader lock across
inbound claims during a send) can be decided alongside the lexicographic fix here, or explicitly
accepted as de-minimis. Documented in ADR-0019.

comments.author: master
comments.text:
LEAN CORRECTED (codex reviewer, PR #50): option (1) as written — a componentwise guard at gateway
SELECTION — does NOT close the reprice race. A gateway that advertises a safe schedule at preflight
and reprices only when upstream `send()` re-fetches `routing_info` is seen with its OLD (safe)
schedule by the selection guard, so the guard passes; option (2) (post-send assert) only detects the
excess AFTER the debit. Neither prevents the INV-1 bypass. To actually PREVENT it, the componentwise
check must run at send()'s refetch/use boundary — which is inside lnv2 `send()`/fedimint, i.e. an
UPSTREAM fix (option 3), not something lnrent can enforce from its own selection layer. So the honest
options are: (3) fix `PaymentFee`'s Ord / send()'s gate to be componentwise upstream in the fork, or
(4) explicitly RETAIN the race and document it (current state, per ADR-0019). The componentwise
SELECTION guard (old option 1) still has value — it stops lnrent from ever PICKING a
low-base/high-ppm gateway visible at preflight — but it must not be described as closing the
send-time reprice race. Revise the acceptance criteria accordingly.
